text: This video describes the Leeds way of performing a standard right hemi-hepatectomy. There are clearly many ways of doing this procedure, but we try to demonstrate an ordered and stepwise approach which is reproducible and safe.
Here we have a seventy year old lady with a large solitary metastasis in the right lobe. She had a bowel resection a year before and received no adjuvant or neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

The surgeon makes a reverse L-shaped skin incision, starting from the xiphysternum and moving vertically down towards the umbilicus, then stopping above the umbilicus and extending transversely and subcostally towards the right.
The surgeon uses a diathermy device to divide the adipose tissue, continuing down to the fascia. The fascia is then divided transversely to identify the linea alba, followed by a vertical incision.

The surgeon uses the Omnitract retractor to apply lateral retraction and lift on the liver. The ligamentum teres is divided and tied off, followed by the division of the falciform ligament, facilitating liver manipulation and rotation.
The surgeon performs the Pringle maneuver by dividing the lesser sac and applying a tape around the hepatic inflow, preparing for intermittent clamping during the liver resection.

During the right hemihepatectomy, the surgeon removes the gallbladder using a fundus-first approach. The cystic duct and artery are identified by pushing surrounding tissue away from the gallbladder. The common bile duct is visible and identifiable, and the cystic duct and artery are tied and divided.
A long tie is placed on the cystic duct, which is then used to apply traction during the dissection of the liver hilum.

Following the removal of the gallbladder, the surgeon dissects the porta hepatis with a sweetheart retractor positioned above it. The assistant lifts the long tie on the cystic duct, allowing the surgeon to use a right angle instrument to dissect vertically towards the portal vein. The peritoneum and lymphatics are divided, and any right accessory artery present is identified.

The portal vein is visible, and attention is directed to the right hepatic artery, located just above the right portal vein. The right hepatic artery is mobilized using a sling. Before dividing the right hepatic artery, the surgeon ensures the presence of a good left hepatic artery, which may be an accessory artery. The right hepatic artery is then divided using strong ties.
The surgeon uses a right angle instrument to dissect vertically downwards between the right and left portal veins, clearly identifying the portal vein bifurcation.

The surgeon mobilizes and divides any chordate process vessels obstructing the right portal vein. A right angle and forceps are used to sling the right portal vein, with dissection directed posteriorly to avoid premature encirclement. The forceps lift the portal vein, revealing the tip of the right angle. The right portal vein is compressed to confirm the line of demarcation along Cantlie's line.
The surgeon divides the right portal vein using an endogia stapler, inserting the thin arm of the stapler into the dissection plane to complete the division.
The surgeon uses fingers to push the chordate process aside to facilitate the placement of the stapler, ensuring that the left portal vein remains uncompromised.

The surgeon mobilizes the right lobe of the liver by dividing the peritoneal reflections along the coronary ligaments and the right triangular ligament. Fingers are used to gently push away the soft tissues after these reflections are divided.

The surgeon uses a right angle instrument to manipulate soft tissue and applies diathermy for cauterization. The hepatocaval ligament is identified and mobilized with the right angle, which is carefully hugged against the cava and pushed vertically until it naturally emerges near the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the right portal vein after slinging it, ensuring safe division of structures within the thick ligament.

The surgeon divides the chordate vessels and focuses on the right hepatic vein. Dissection is performed superiorly between the right and middle hepatic veins using a right angle instrument. The liver is flipped over, and dissection continues in the same area, directed towards the patient's left shoulder. Hydrostatic pressure is applied to identify the pathway, avoiding encircling the right hepatic vein to prevent damage.

The surgeon directs the right angle instrument towards the patient's left shoulder to pass a sling around the right hepatic vein. Space is created around the vein to accommodate the endogia stapler, which is carefully positioned against the liver to prevent narrowing of the vena cava.

The surgeon begins the transection of the liver, following a dotted line that indicates Cantley's line. The incision is made approximately one centimeter to the right of Cantley's line, aiming to cut through a devascularized section of the liver.
The surgeon applies retraction to the left using a clip on the falciform ligament. The liver is divided using the CUSA device in combination with diathermy, facilitating precise tissue separation.

The surgeon divides the liver capsule using the tip of the CUSA in a pushing motion, followed by ultrasonic aspiration with a stroking technique. A vein becomes visible, and clips are applied using a rolling technique to secure the structure. The surgeon uses the tip of the CUSA to burn small structures after full dissection, avoiding deep holes in the liver to maximize access and efficiency in managing bleeding vessels.

Bleeding occurs on the cut surface of the liver, which is controlled using argon coagulation. The Pringle maneuver is applied, alternating between ten minutes of occlusion and five minutes of release, followed by fifteen minutes of occlusion and five minutes of release.

The surgeon uses a stapler gun to divide the anterior and posterior pedicles, minimizing anatomical distortion and ensuring a reliable seal. The chordate process is visible and must be divided to access the posterior pedicle. The stapler is applied, and additional clips may be necessary if the pedicle is wide.
The surgeon completes the final part of the liver transection after dividing both pedicles. Veins at the posterior aspect are identified and tied before the liver specimen is removed.

After the specimen is removed, the surgeon uses diathermy or argon on the cut liver surface to achieve hemostasis, ensuring not to affect any ties or sutures. A swab is placed on the cut surface to check for bile leaks, which are then repaired with sutures if detected.
A Robinson drain is placed on the cut surface of the liver following major resection. Surgical glue is optionally applied to the cut surface for additional sealing.
After completing the right hemiepitectomy, the surgeon reconstructs the falciform ligament to stabilize the remnant liver and prevent torsion.
The surgeon completes the mask closure using PDS sutures, followed by closing the skin with either staples or sutures, marking the end of the liver resection procedure.
